audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman phoning the secretary of a cycling club to find out about becoming a member.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, South City Cycling Club.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Oh, hi.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Um. I want to find out about joining the club.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right, I can help you there.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: I'm the club secretary, and my name's Jim Hunter.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Oh, hi, Jim.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: The club secretary's name is Jim Hunter, so Hunter has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, South City Cycling Club.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Oh, hi.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Um. I want to find out about joining the club.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Right, I can help you there.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: I'm the club secretary, and my name's Jim Hunter.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Oh, hi, Jim.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So, are you interested in membership for yourself?
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, well there are basically two types of adult membership.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: If you're pretty serious about cycling, there's the full membership.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: That costs $260, and that covers you not just for ordinary cycling, but also for races both here in the city and also in other parts of Australia.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well I'm not really up to that standard.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I was more interested in just joining a group to do some cycling in my free time.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sure, that's why most people join.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: So, in that case, you'd be better with the recreational membership.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: That's $108 if you're over 19, and ninety five dollars if you're under.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: It's paid quarterly, and you can upgrade it later to the full membership if you want to, of course.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Now both types of membership include the club fee of $20.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: They also provide insurance in case you have an accident, though we hope you won't need that, of course.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: No, OK.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, I'll go with the recreational membership, I think.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And that allows me to join in the club activities and so on.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: And once you're a member of the club, you're also permitted to wear our kit when you're out cycling.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: It's green and white.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Yes, I've seen cyclists wearing it.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So can I buy that at the club?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. No, it's made to order by a company in Brisbane.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: You can find them online.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: They're called Jerriz.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: That's JERRIZ.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: You can use your membership number to put in an order on their website.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: OK, now can you tell me a bit about the rides I can do?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sure, so we have training rides pretty well every morning, and they're a really good way of improving your cycling skills as well as your general level of fitness.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: But they're different levels.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Level A is pretty fast, you're looking at about 30 or 35 kilometers an hour.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: If you can do about 25 km an hour, you'd probably be level B, and then level C are the novices who stay at about 15 km per hour.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, I reckon I'd be level B.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So when are the sessions for that level?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. There are a couple each week.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: They're both early morning sessions.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: There's one on Tuesdays, and for that one you meet at 5:30 am.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: And the meeting point's the stadium.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Do you know where that is?
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Yes, it's quite near my home, in fact.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, and how about the other one?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's on Thursdays, it starts at the same time, but they meet at the main gate to the park.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Is that the one just past the shopping mall?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's it.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So how long are the rides?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. They're about an hour and a half.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: So if you have a job, it's easy to fit in before you go to work.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: And the members often go somewhere for coffee afterwards, so it's quite a social event.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: OK, that sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I've only just moved to the city, so I don't actually know many people yet.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, it's a great way to meet people.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: And does each ride have a leader?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Sometimes, but not always.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: But you don't really need one.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: The group members on the ride support one another anyway.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: How would we know where to go?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: If you check the club website, you'll see that the route for each ride is clearly marked.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: So you can just print that out and take it along with you.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: It's similar from one week to another, but it's not always exactly the same.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: And what do I need to bring?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Hmm. Well, bring a bottle of water and your phone.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: You shouldn't use it while you're cycling, but have it with you.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And in winter, it's well before sunrise when we set out, so you need to make sure your bike's got lights.
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: That's OK.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, thanks, Jim.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'd definitely like to join.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So what's the best way of going about it?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. You can...
audioscript.speaker: JIM

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Further information

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a manager in a company talking to employees about volunteering for charity work.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Thanks for coming everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, so this meeting is for new staff and staff who haven't been involved with our volunteering projects yet.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So basically the idea is that we allow staff to give up some of their work time to help on various charity projects to benefit the local community.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We've been doing this for the last five years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it's been very successful.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Participating doesn't necessarily involve a huge time commitment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The company will pay for 8 hours of your time. That can be used over one or two days, all at once, or spread over several months throughout the year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are some staff who enjoy volunteering so much, they also give up their own free time for a couple of hours every week.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's completely up to you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Obviously, many people will have family commitments and aren't as available as other members of staff.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Feedback from staff has been overwhelmingly positive, because they felt they were doing something really useful.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Nearly everyone agreed that volunteering made them feel more motivated at work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They also liked building relationships with the people in the local community, and felt valued by them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One or two people also said it was a good thing to have on their CVs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One particularly successful project last year was the Get Working project.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This was aimed at helping unemployed people in the area get back to work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Our staff were able to help them improve their telephone skills, such as writing down messages and speaking with confidence to potential customers, which they had found quite difficult.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is something many employers look for in job applicants, and something we all do without even thinking about every day at work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We've got an exciting new project starting this year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Up until now, we've mainly focused on projects to do with education and training, and we'll continue with our reading project in schools.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And our work with local charities, but we've also agreed to help out on a conservation project in Redfern Park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, if any of you fancy being outside and getting your hands dirty, this is the project for you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I also want to mention the annual Digital Inclusion Day, which is coming up next month.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The aim of this is to help older people keep up with technology.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And this year, instead of hosting the event in our own training facility, we're using the ICT suite at Hill College, as it can hold far more people.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We've invited over 60 people from the Silver Age Community Center to take part, so we'll need a lot of volunteers to help with this event.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you're interested in taking part, please go to the volunteering section of our website and complete the relevant form.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We won't be providing any training for this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But you'll be paired with an experienced volunteer if you've never done it before.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By the way, don't forget to tell your manager about any volunteering activities you decide to do.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: The participants on the Digital Inclusion Day really benefited.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The majority were in their 70s, though some were younger, and a few were even in their 90s.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Quite a few owned both a computer and a mobile phone, but these tended to be outdated models.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They generally knew how to do simple things like send texts, but weren't aware of recent developments in mobile phone technology.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A few were keen to learn, but most were quite dismissive at first.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They couldn't see the point of updating their skills, but that soon changed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The feedback was very positive.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The really encouraging thing was that participants all said they felt much more confident about using social media to keep in touch with their grandchildren, who prefer this form of communication to phoning or sending emails.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A lot of them also said playing online games would help them make new friends and keep their brains active.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They weren't that impressed with being able to order their groceries online.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As they liked going out to the shops, but some said it would come in handy if they were ill or the weather was really bad.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One thing they asked about was using tablets for things like reading newspapers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some people had been given tablets as presents, but had never used them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So that's something we'll make sure we include this time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 11 to 16.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Thanks for coming everyone.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK, so this meeting is for new staff and staff who haven't been involved with our volunteering projects yet.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So basically the idea is that we allow staff to give up some of their work time to help on various charity projects to benefit the local community.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We've been doing this for the last five years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And it's been very successful.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Participating doesn't necessarily involve a huge time commitment.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The company will pay for 8 hours of your time. That can be used over one or two days, all at once, or spread over several months throughout the year.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are some staff who enjoy volunteering so much, they also give up their own free time for a couple of hours every week.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's completely up to you.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Obviously, many people will have family commitments and aren't as available as other members of staff.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Feedback from staff has been overwhelmingly positive, because they felt they were doing something really useful.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Nearly everyone agreed that volunteering made them feel more motivated at work.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They also liked building relationships with the people in the local community, and felt valued by them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One or two people also said it was a good thing to have on their CVs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One particularly successful project last year was the Get Working project.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This was aimed at helping unemployed people in the area get back to work.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Our staff were able to help them improve their telephone skills, such as writing down messages and speaking with confidence to potential customers, which they had found quite difficult.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is something many employers look for in job applicants, and something we all do without even thinking about every day at work.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We've got an exciting new project starting this year.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Up until now, we've mainly focused on projects to do with education and training, and we'll continue with our reading project in schools.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And our work with local charities, but we've also agreed to help out on a conservation project in Redfern Park.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, if any of you fancy being outside and getting your hands dirty, this is the project for you.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I also want to mention the annual Digital Inclusion Day, which is coming up next month.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The aim of this is to help older people keep up with technology.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And this year, instead of hosting the event in our own training facility, we're using the ICT suite at Hill College, as it can hold far more people.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We've invited over 60 people from the Silver Age Community Center to take part, so we'll need a lot of volunteers to help with this event.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you're interested in taking part, please go to the volunteering section of our website and complete the relevant form.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We won't be providing any training for this.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But you'll be paired with an experienced volunteer if you've never done it before.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: By the way, don't forget to tell your manager about any volunteering activities you decide to do.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: The participants on the Digital Inclusion Day really benefited.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The majority were in their 70s, though some were younger, and a few were even in their 90s.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Quite a few owned both a computer and a mobile phone, but these tended to be outdated models.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They generally knew how to do simple things like send texts, but weren't aware of recent developments in mobile phone technology.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A few were keen to learn, but most were quite dismissive at first.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They couldn't see the point of updating their skills, but that soon changed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The feedback was very positive.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The really encouraging thing was that participants all said they felt much more confident about using social media to keep in touch with their grandchildren, who prefer this form of communication to phoning or sending emails.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A lot of them also said playing online games would help them make new friends and keep their brains active.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They weren't that impressed with being able to order their groceries online.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As they liked going out to the shops, but some said it would come in handy if they were ill or the weather was really bad.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One thing they asked about was using tablets for things like reading newspapers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some people had been given tablets as presents, but had never used them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So that's something we'll make sure we include this time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: How much time for volunteering does the company allow per employee?
In feedback almost all employees said that volunteering improved their
Last year some staff helped unemployed people with their
This year the company will start a new volunteering project with a local
Where will the Digital Inclusion Day be held?
What should staff do if they want to take part in the Digital Inclusion Day?

body.title: Which TWO things are mentioned about the participants on the last Digital Inclusion Day?
options: They only used their phones for making calls.

body.title: Which TWO activities on the last Digital Inclusion Day did participants describe as useful?

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a student called Russ consulting his tutor about a presentation he is preparing on nanotechnology, the study of materials on an extremely small scale.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Ah... come in, Russ.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: Now you wanted to consult me about your class presentation on nanotechnology.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You're due to give it next week, aren't you?
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That's right, and I'm really struggling.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: I chose the topic because I didn't know much about it, and wanted to learn more.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: But now I've read so much about it, in a way there's too much to say.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: I could talk for much longer than the 20 minutes I've been allocated.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: Should I assume the other students don't know much, and give them a kind of general introduction, or should I try and make them share my fascination with a particular aspect?
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: You could do either, but you'll need to have it clear in your own mind.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Then I think I'll give an overview.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: OK, now one way of approaching this is to work through developments in chronological order.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: On the other hand, you could talk about the numerous ways that nanotechnology is being applied.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You mean things like thin films on camera displays to make them water-repellent,
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: and additives to make motorcycle helmets stronger and lighter.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Or another way would be to focus on its impact in one particular area.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Say medicine or space exploration.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That would make it easier to focus.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: Perhaps I should do that.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: I think that would be a good idea.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: How important is it to include slides in the presentation?
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: They aren't essential by any means, and there's a danger of tailoring what you say to fit whatever slides you can find.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: While it can be good to include slides, you could end up spending too long looking for suitable ones.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You might find it better to leave them out.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: Another thing I was wondering about was how to start.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: I know presentations often begin with, first, I'm going to talk about this, and then I'll talk about that.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: But I thought about asking the audience what they know about nanotechnology.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: That would be fine if you had an hour or two for the presentation, but you might find that you can't do anything with the answers you get.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: And it simply eats into the short time that's available.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: So maybe I should mention a particular way that nanotechnology is used to focus people's attention.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: That sounds sensible.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: What do you think I should do next?
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: I really have to plan the presentation today and tomorrow.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: Well, initially, I think you should ignore all the notes you've made.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Take a small piece of paper and write a single short sentence that ties together the whole presentation.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: It can be something as simple as, nanotechnology is already improving our lives.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Then start planning the content around that.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You can always modify that sentence later if you need to.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, now let's think about actually giving the presentation.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You've only given one before, if I remember correctly, about an experiment you've been involved in.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: It was pretty rubbish.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: Let's say it was better in some respects than in others.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: With regard to the structure, I felt that you ended rather abruptly without rounding it off.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Be careful not to do that in next week's presentation.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: And you made very little eye contact with the audience, because you were looking down at your notes most of the time.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You need to be looking at the audience and only occasionally glancing at your notes.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Hmm. Your body language was a little odd.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Every time you showed a slide, you turned your back on the audience, so you could look at it.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You should have been looking at your laptop, and you kept scratching your head, so I found myself wondering when you were next going to do that, instead of listening to what you were saying.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Oh, dear.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: What did you think of the language?
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: I knew that not everyone was familiar with the subject, so I tried to make it as simple as I could.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: Yes, that came across.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: You used a few words that are specific to the field, but you always explained what they meant, so the audience wouldn't have had any difficulty understanding.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Uh-huh.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: I must say the handouts you prepared were well thought out.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: They were a good summary of your presentation, which people would have been able to refer to later on.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: So well done on that.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: Well, I hope that helps you with next week's presentation.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: Yes, it will.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: Thanks a lot.
audioscript.speaker: RUSS

audioscript.text: I'll look forward to seeing a big improvement then.
audioscript.speaker: TUTOR

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Russ says that his difficulty in planning the presentation is due to
options: his lack of knowledge about the topic.
his uncertainty about what he should try to achieve.
the short time that he has for preparation.

items.title: Russ and his tutor agree that his approach in the presentation will be
options: to concentrate on how nanotechnology is used in one field.
to follow the chronological development of nanotechnology.
to show the range of applications of nanotechnology.

items.title: In connection with slides, the tutor advises Russ to
options: talk about things that he can find slides to illustrate.
look for slides to illustrate the points he makes.

items.title: They both agree that the best way for Russ to start his presentation is
options: to encourage the audience to talk.
to explain what Russ intends to do.

items.title: What does the tutor advise Russ to do next while preparing his presentation?
options: summarise the main point he wants to make
read the notes he has already made
list the topics he wants to cover

text: What comments do the speakers make about each of the following aspects of Russ's previous presentation?

body.title: Aspects of Russ's previous presentation

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a lecturer in psychology talking about a type of memory.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Today, we'll be continuing the series of lectures on memory by focusing on what is called episodic memory, and what can happen if this is not working properly.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Episodic memory refers to the memory of an event or episode.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Episodic memories allow us to mentally travel back in time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To an event from the past.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Episodic memories include various details about these events, for example, when an event happened.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And other information, such as the location.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To help understand this concept, try to remember the last time you ate dinner at a restaurant.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The ability to remember where you ate, who you were with and the items you ordered are all features of an episodic memory.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Episodic memory is distinct from another type of memory called semantic memory.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is the type of factual memory.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That we have in common with everyone else.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is your general knowledge of the world.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To build upon a previous example, remembering where you parked your car is an example of episodic memory, but your understanding of what a car is.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And how an engine works are examples of semantic memory.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Unlike episodic memory, semantic memory isn't dependent on recalling personal experiences.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Episodic memory can be thought of as a process with several different steps of memory processing: encoding, consolidation and retrieval.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The initial step is called encoding.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This involves the process of receiving and registering information, which is necessary for creating memories of information or events that you experience.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The degree to which you can successfully encode information depends on the level of attention you give to an event while it's actually happening.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Being distracted can make effective encoding very difficult.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Encoding of episodic memories is also influenced by how you process the event.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, if you were introduced to someone called Charlie.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You might make the connection that your uncle has the same name.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Future recollection of Charlie's name is much easier.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you have a strategy to help you encode it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Memory consolidation, the next step in forming an episodic memory, is the process by which memories of encoded information are strengthened, stabilized, and stored to facilitate later retrieval.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Consolidation is most effective when the information being stored can be linked to an existing network of information.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Consolidation makes it possible for you to store memories for later retrieval indefinitely.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Forming strong memories depends on the frequency with which you try to retrieve them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Memories can fade, or become harder to retrieve if they aren't used very often.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The last step in forming episodic memories is called retrieval, which is the conscious recollection of encoded information.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Retrieving information from episodic memory depends upon semantic, olfactory, auditory, and visual factors.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These help episodic memory retrieval by acting as a prompt, for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When recalling where you parked your car, you may use the color of a sign close to where you parked.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You actually have to mentally travel back to the moment you parked.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are a wide range of neurological diseases and conditions that can affect episodic memory.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These range from Alzheimer's.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To schizophrenia, to autism.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: An impairment of episodic memory can have a profound effect on individuals' lives, for example.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The symptoms of schizophrenia can be reasonably well controlled by medication.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, patients' episodic memory may still be impaired, and so they are often unable to return to university or work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Recent studies have shown that computer-assisted games designed to keep the brain active can help improve their episodic memory.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Episodic memories can help people connect with others, for instance, by sharing intimate details about their past.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Something, individuals with autism often have problems with.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This may be caused by an absence of a sense of self.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is essential for the storage of episodic memory, and has been found to be impaired in children with autism.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Research has shown that treatments that improve memory may also have a positive impact on children's social development.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One study looked at...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear a lecturer in psychology talking about a type of memory.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Today, we'll be continuing the series of lectures on memory by focusing on what is called episodic memory, and what can happen if this is not working properly.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Episodic memory refers to the memory of an event or episode.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Episodic memories allow us to mentally travel back in time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To an event from the past.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Episodic memories include various details about these events, for example, when an event happened.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And other information, such as the location.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To help understand this concept, try to remember the last time you ate dinner at a restaurant.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The ability to remember where you ate, who you were with and the items you ordered are all features of an episodic memory.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Episodic memory is distinct from another type of memory called semantic memory.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is the type of factual memory.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That we have in common with everyone else.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is your general knowledge of the world.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To build upon a previous example, remembering where you parked your car is an example of episodic memory, but your understanding of what a car is.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And how an engine works are examples of semantic memory.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Unlike episodic memory, semantic memory isn't dependent on recalling personal experiences.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Episodic memory can be thought of as a process with several different steps of memory processing: encoding, consolidation and retrieval.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The initial step is called encoding.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This involves the process of receiving and registering information, which is necessary for creating memories of information or events that you experience.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The degree to which you can successfully encode information depends on the level of attention you give to an event while it's actually happening.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Being distracted can make effective encoding very difficult.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Encoding of episodic memories is also influenced by how you process the event.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, if you were introduced to someone called Charlie.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You might make the connection that your uncle has the same name.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Future recollection of Charlie's name is much easier.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you have a strategy to help you encode it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Memory consolidation, the next step in forming an episodic memory, is the process by which memories of encoded information are strengthened, stabilized, and stored to facilitate later retrieval.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Consolidation is most effective when the information being stored can be linked to an existing network of information.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Consolidation makes it possible for you to store memories for later retrieval indefinitely.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Forming strong memories depends on the frequency with which you try to retrieve them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Memories can fade, or become harder to retrieve if they aren't used very often.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The last step in forming episodic memories is called retrieval, which is the conscious recollection of encoded information.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Retrieving information from episodic memory depends upon semantic, olfactory, auditory, and visual factors.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These help episodic memory retrieval by acting as a prompt, for example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When recalling where you parked your car, you may use the color of a sign close to where you parked.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You actually have to mentally travel back to the moment you parked.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are a wide range of neurological diseases and conditions that can affect episodic memory.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These range from Alzheimer's.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To schizophrenia, to autism.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: An impairment of episodic memory can have a profound effect on individuals' lives, for example.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The symptoms of schizophrenia can be reasonably well controlled by medication.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, patients' episodic memory may still be impaired, and so they are often unable to return to university or work.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Recent studies have shown that computer-assisted games designed to keep the brain active can help improve their episodic memory.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Episodic memories can help people connect with others, for instance, by sharing intimate details about their past.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Something, individuals with autism often have problems with.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This may be caused by an absence of a sense of self.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is essential for the storage of episodic memory, and has been found to be impaired in children with autism.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Research has shown that treatments that improve memory may also have a positive impact on children's social development.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One study looked at...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
